L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during terrain flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was proximity flying a technical line in a wingsuit and impacted terrain in terminal freefall, striking through a small tree/bush before coming to rest 50m downhill. The evidence indicates he lost adequate speed and/or glide performance during the flight, failing to maintain sufficient energy and separation from terrain to clear a technical section of the line. His canopy was found open at the impact site, suggesting a late emergency deployment attempt that was insufficient to arrest the descent.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew line requiring terrain clearance without margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flight line that required clearing a ledge, at a site known by only a very few jumpers. She was unable to outfly the ledge with her wingsuit, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall. The decision to fly a line with insufficient margin over/past a terrain feature was the producing mechanism — the narrative states she 'couldn't outfly a ledge with her wings' and did not attempt to pull, indicating she was committed to a line that left no recovery option when performance fell short of what was required.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to tumble
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Francesco had an unstable exit and tumbled for approximately 6 seconds before performing an emergency pull. The unstable exit resulted in prolonged tumbling that consumed freefall time without building forward separation from the cliff, and the emergency deployment from a compromised body position produced a 180-degree off-heading opening that caused fatal object strike. The jumper intended to execute a normal slider-up cliff jump but failed in the physical execution of the exit, which was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew unknown technical proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to fly a technical proximity line through a notch on his first visit to the object, without laser data, GPS data, or prior flights to assess whether his wingsuit (Freak 2) could clear the terrain. He was explicitly told the correct way to assess the line required laser and GPS data he did not have, was told the companion did not know if a Freak 2 could make it, and was offered safer alternative flight paths. This decision to commit to an unverified technical proximity line with acknowledged uncertainty about its feasibility was the producing mechanism for the terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Over-rotation on aerial exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper intended to perform 3 gainers followed by a track for separation, a maneuver he had previously executed successfully at this site. On this jump he over-rotated the last gainer, ending up in a standing position still rotating, which forced a recovery sequence (turning to face away from the cliff) that consumed critical time and altitude. This resulted in insufficient forward separation from the cliff at deployment, leading to object strike during canopy inflation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydive gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used skydiving equipment for a BASE jump from an antenna. Skydiving gear is designed for terminal-velocity deployments with thousands of feet of altitude margin and is fundamentally inappropriate for BASE jumping, where sub-terminal slider-up deployments require BASE-specific pilot chute sizes, bridle lengths, and container configurations to achieve rapid canopy extraction. The skydive gear sniveled to impact, which is the predictable consequence of deploying skydiving equipment at sub-terminal airspeeds from a fixed object with insufficient altitude for the prolonged opening sequence.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy at a reasonably high altitude but was unable to locate the pilot chute in the BOC pocket. Camera footage shows the jumper flipped over during the pull attempt and continued until impact. This is an execution failure — the jumper intended to deploy but failed to physically extract the PC, resulting in a total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped object with insufficient clearance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump from the second level of the Eiffel Tower at 370 feet, with the first level platform protruding at 200 feet AGL — only 170 feet below the exit point. This structure geometry meant the jumper had to clear a massive horizontal obstruction with minimal vertical separation. The decision to jump from this level, after the higher exit was ruled out and the second jumper elected not to jump, was clearly and specifically inadequate: a 170-foot slider-down BOC deployment from a structure with a large protruding platform directly below required near-perfect execution with essentially zero margin for any delay or sub-optimal trajectory, and the object's geometry made impact with the lower level a foreseeable and dominant risk. The jumper impacted the first level, confirming the exit point selection was the producing mechanism of the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and altitude during flight corrections
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper made a drastic 90° turn to the right to avoid a ledge, then corrected 90° back to the left. These aggressive maneuvers bled speed and altitude, leaving him flying very low and slow with insufficient energy to clear the powerlines ahead. The turns resulted in a loss of speed-glide that he could not recover from, producing the fatal terrain/obstacle impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched head down, lost control, and deployed unstable from a slider-up cliff on his first BASE jump. The unstable deployment resulted in an off-heading opening that led to fatal object strike. The execution of the exit and deployment was inadequate despite selecting a course of action (jumping the cliff), making this a performance/skill-based error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed brake release after correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After experiencing a 90° off-heading opening to the left, the jumper corrected heading with rear risers but took 'precious seconds' to release his brakes. This delayed transition from riser correction to brake flight cost critical altitude and forward distance, leaving him too low to clear the dangerous water pool. The narrative explicitly notes the delay in brake release as the key execution problem.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued to main LZ when too low
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After losing considerable altitude during the heading correction, the jumper decided to continue flying forward toward the main landing zone rather than landing in the closer emergency landing areas to the left or right. The narrative states he was already low but 'decided to go forward for the main landing zone' and could not clear the pool, landing in the dangerous water. Given the known lethal hazard of the pool with its strong water stream, continuing forward when already low rather than selecting a closer emergency option was an identifiably inappropriate decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping with no training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, and no experienced jumper with him. He purchased BASE gear and attempted a BASE jump from an under-hung cliff location without any of the prerequisite skydiving experience, BASE first jump course, or mentorship that constitutes accepted practice. This decision to attempt a BASE jump with zero preparation was the producing mechanism for the fatality — no amount of execution quality could have compensated for the complete absence of the skills required to survive the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper pivoted off the launch, went on his back, and struck the cliff in freefall. He failed to achieve any stable body position or forward separation from the under-hung cliff face. This execution failure was the direct mechanism through which he impacted the cliff subterminal near the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Skydive gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jean-Marc used skydiving equipment (a Vega 180 skydiving reserve canopy packed in a deployment bag) for a BASE jump from a cliff (Angel Falls). Using skydive gear—specifically a small skydiving reserve canopy with a deployment bag rather than proper BASE equipment—is a clearly inappropriate equipment decision for a cliff jump. The deployment bag configuration and small canopy with poor heading performance characteristics directly contributed to the 180° off-heading opening and line twists that produced the fatal wall strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was in a back-to-earth position filming two other jumpers and extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in a low pull. He impacted the talus while his canopy was still opening. The focus on filming caused an attention failure that led to loss of altitude awareness and delayed deployment beyond the point of survivability.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC of his new LD5 container, which had a different BOC placement than his previous Zack rig. The witness observed him reaching backwards unsuccessfully, after which he destabilized (dipped right shoulder, pitched head-down), and by the time the canopy began to extract he was too low for it to inflate. The failed execution of the deployment sequence directly produced the fatal low-altitude impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stowed PC on static line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to place the pilot chute into the BOC pouch instead of leaving it out as required for a static line deployment. This decision directly caused the malfunction: when the static line became taut, it applied force at a right angle to the stowed PC, causing the break cord to part prematurely before extracting the PC and bridle, resulting in a total malfunction. The domain reference explicitly states that the PC must never be stowed during a static line jump.
